mnemonics.title: Gram-Schmidt Steps
mnemonics.content: GRAM: G - Get first vector, R - Remove projection from second, A - And remove from all remaining, M - Make orthogonal set. First vector stays, subtract projections from each subsequent vector.

mnemonics.title: Orthogonal vs Orthonormal
mnemonics.content: ORTHOGONAL: 'ORTHO' means right angle, vectors at 90°. ORTHONORMAL: 'ORTHO' + 'NORMAL' (unit length), right angle AND unit length.

mnemonics.title: Projection Formula
mnemonics.content: Project u on v = (u·v / v·v) × v. Think: 'UV over VV times v' - numerator is dot product, denominator is norm squared.

acronyms.expansion: Q has Orthonormal columns, R is Upper triangular
acronyms.usage: QR Decomposition: A = QR where Q^TQ = I

acronyms.expansion: Special Orthogonal group - n×n matrices with det = +1
acronyms.usage: Rotation matrices in n dimensions, orthogonal with determinant 1
All orthogonal matrices, includes reflections with det = -1

visualTips.visualization: Draw two perpendicular lines like an L-shape or cross. In 3D, think of x, y, z axes all meeting at 90° angles.
Imagine starting with non-orthogonal vectors as 'crooked' lines. Each step 'straightens' them by removing the tilt caused by previous vectors, like squaring up a picture frame.
An orthogonal matrix represents a rotation (det=+1) or reflection (det=-1). It preserves lengths and angles - the 'shape' of the space doesn't change.
Projection of u onto v is like the shadow of u on a line in the direction of v, scaled to land exactly on the line.

analogies.analogy: Like having coordinate axes that are not skewed - you can locate any point easily by knowing how far along each axis, without complicated calculations.
Like organizing a group of people into pairs where each person faces a different direction - making them 'independent' in their directions.
If W is a subspace like a floor (2D plane), W⊥ is the ceiling direction (vertical line) - everything perpendicular to the floor points straight up.
Like a rotation dial or mirror - it changes orientation but preserves all distances, like a rigid body rotation in space.
